frame_analyses.description: First instruments enter the field and bring the needle-suture toward the Penrose; this is the likely timing start.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Controlled entry, but there is a brief setup period before the bite sequence begins.

frame_analyses.description: The needle has traversed the slit edges and the suture is being drawn through both sides of the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Bites appear close to the target marks, though exact mark centers are partly obscured by angle and instruments.

frame_analyses.description: A double-wrap first throw is being formed around the opposing strand for the surgeon's knot.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Loop geometry is acceptable, but the first knot takes extra regrasping and time before it seats.

frame_analyses.description: After the first knot seats, the operator reorients and begins the next single throw with opposite-hand setup.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Hand switch appears present; knot placement remains centered without avulsing the drain.

frame_analyses.description: The third-throw interval begins with the free end elevated and loop geometry re-established over the knot stack.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The required alternating pattern appears to continue, but this phase is prolonged by repeated repositioning.

frame_analyses.description: The required third throw appears seated by this portion of the case, followed by additional reinforcing manipulation before cutting.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Knot security looks acceptable, but extra throws/manipulation cost substantial time.

frame_analyses.description: A cutting instrument is applied to the suture tails inside the trainer.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Tail cutting is performed intracorporeally as required.

frame_analyses.description: Both tails appear cut and the instruments withdraw from the field.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task appears complete without drain avulsion or knot failure.

knot_assessments.notes: Best estimate is a right-instrument-led double throw consistent with a surgeon's knot; it ultimately seats without obvious slip.
A left-instrument-led single throw appears to follow the first throw, with visible hand alternation but some extra setup time.
The third required throw appears right-instrument-led and secure; additional reinforcing throws/manipulation seem to follow before cutting.

technique_summary: The task is completed successfully with controlled tissue handling, no drain avulsion, and a knot stack that appears secure. The main performance limiter is efficiency: the first throw takes time to seat, and there appear to be additional reinforcing throws/manipulations after the required third throw before the tails are cut. Suture placement looks close to the marks, but exact deviation is low-confidence because the targets are intermittently obscured.
improvement_suggestions: After the third required throw is seated, transition directly to cutting rather than adding extra reinforcing throws unless the knot is clearly unstable.
Keep the post and working end more clearly separated during hand switches to reduce regrasping and shorten the second and third throw setup.
Load and drive the needle with a more consistent bite depth aimed directly through the marked targets to tighten mark accuracy.
strengths: Completed the task without drain avulsion and cut both suture ends inside the trainer.
The first throw appears to be a proper double throw, and the first three required throws appear to alternate hands.
Instrument control is generally steady, with gentle handling of the Penrose and no obvious knot slippage.
